text: Write a plain-prose story of about 600 words describing a day in the life of a lighthouse keeper on a remote island. No tools, no code blocks, no headings - just flowing prose, and do not stop early.

The morning arrived not with an alarm but with the slow brightening of the eastern sky, a pale wash of gray that crept through the narrow window of the keeper's quarters and fell across the wool blanket in a thin stripe. Thomas opened his eyes and lay still for a moment, listening. The wind had died overnight, and in its absence the world seemed impossibly quiet, save for the low murmur of waves folding themselves against the rocks below. He swung his legs off the cot, set his feet on the cold stone floor, and began the day the same way he had begun every day for the past eleven years.

First, the light. He climbed the spiral staircase, seventy-two steps of cast iron that rang faintly under his boots, up through the narrow throat of the tower until he emerged into the lantern room. The Fresnel lens sat massive and silent, its concentric glass rings catching the first daylight and throwing small rainbows against the walls. He checked the mechanism, wound the clockwork that governed the rotation, inspected the wick and the oil reservoir. Everything was in order. It almost always was, but the checking was the point. Routine was not the enemy of attention. It was the guardian of it.

Back down in the kitchen, he boiled water on the small iron stove and made coffee with grounds he rationed carefully. The supply boat came once every three weeks, and he was ten days from the next visit. He ate two slices of bread with salted butter and a hard-boiled egg, standing at the window that faced the mainland. On clear days he could see the faint suggestion of the coast, a dark smudge resting on the horizon like a thumbprint. Today the air was hazy, and the sea and sky merged into a single unbroken field of silver.

After breakfast he walked the perimeter of the island. It took forty minutes at an unhurried pace. The island was barely a quarter mile across, a rough hump of granite and scrub grass battered into shape by centuries of Atlantic weather. He checked the outbuildings, the fuel shed, the small boathouse where his dory sat on its cradle. He noted the condition of the paths, the erosion along the southern cliff, the colony of gulls that had taken up residence on the western rocks. He paused there for a time and watched them, their white bodies wheeling against the overcast sky, their cries sharp and repetitive. He had stopped finding them annoying years ago. Now they were simply part of the texture of things, like the sound of the wind or the smell of salt.

The middle of the day he spent on maintenance. He scraped rust from the railing on the gallery deck, repainted a section of the tower wall, repaired a hinge on the storage room door. The work was unglamorous and repetitive, but it suited him. There was a particular satisfaction in keeping something functional, in holding back the slow entropy of weather and time through nothing more than consistent effort. He ate lunch alone, as he ate every meal, and afterward sat in his chair by the window and read for an hour from a battered paperback novel he had already read twice before.

In the late afternoon he climbed the tower again to prepare the light for the coming darkness. He trimmed the wick, filled the reservoir, polished a smudge from one of the lens panels with a soft cloth. As the sun dropped toward the water, he lit the flame and watched it catch and grow, watched the lens begin its slow rotation, throwing its beam out across the darkening sea. He stood in the lantern room and felt the familiar quiet pride of it, this simple duty performed with care, this small fire burning against the vast indifferent night.

He ate supper, washed his plate, and sat outside on the bench by the door as the stars emerged one by one. The beam swept steadily overhead, marking its patient arc. Somewhere out on that dark water, perhaps, a ship was turning toward safety because of this light. Perhaps not. It did not matter. The light burned regardless, and Thomas kept it burning, and that was enough.
